{"parentUuid":null,"isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":"I need EXACT technical specifications for the CShapes 2.0 dataset. Search the web for:\n\n1. What CRS/projection does CShapes 2.0 use? (I expect WGS84 / EPSG:4326)\n2. What geometry type? (MULTIPOLYGON?)\n3. Does CShapes include DEPENDENT territories (colonies, protectorates) or ONLY sovereign states?\n4. Search the CShapes 2.0 paper: \"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset\" by Schvitz et al. (2022) in Journal of Conflict Resolution.\n5. What coding system does CShapes use? (Gleditsch-Ward codes? COW codes? Both?)\n6. The CShapes R package (`cshapes::cshp()`) - what columns does it return?\n7. Specifically: does CShapes have a \"status\" or \"type\" field that distinguishes sovereign states from dependencies?\n\nAlso check:\n8. What CRS does GADM use?\n9. What CRS does Natural Earth use?\n10. What CRS does Aourednik Historical Basemaps use?\n\nI need to know if polygons from GADM/Natural Earth/Aourednik can be DIRECTLY combined with CShapes polygons, or if CRS transformations are needed. This is for an R/sf-based pipeline."},"uuid":"1e7c14db-9032-4f49-a82e-1224fa6c6b12","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:09.291Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

{"parentUuid":"c92bd38e-0c09-45a8-b889-5142b324a235","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"progress","data":{"type":"hook_progress","hookEvent":"PostToolUse","hookName":"PostToolUse:Read","command":"callback"},"parentToolUseID":"toolu_01GkriMRnnUAh3steCb6vTt2","toolUseID":"toolu_01GkriMRnnUAh3steCb6vTt2","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:27.768Z","uuid":"c76fd2ee-b8bf-4bcd-aaa3-689228b2d9ff","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"996c06bb-00ff-457f-97ac-a09171910bc0","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01GkriMRnnUAh3steCb6vTt2","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Only for running when integrating with CShapes data for first time\n     2→\n     3→polities_inputs_path <- here::here(\"data-raw\", \"polities_inputs\")\n     4→\n     5→cshapes_renames <- here::here() |>\n     6→  fs::path(polities_inputs_path, \"rename_cshapes.csv\") |>\n     7→  readr::read_csv()\n     8→\n     9→cshapes <- k_cshapes |>\n    10→  tibble::as_tibble() |>\n    11→  dplyr::arrange(polity_name, end_year) |>\n    12→  dplyr::inner_join(\n    13→    cshapes_renames,\n    14→    by = dplyr::join_by(polity_name == country_name),\n    15→    unmatched = \"error\"\n    16→  ) |>\n    17→  dplyr::mutate(\n    18→    original_name = .add_years_in_name(polity_name, start_year, end_year),\n    19→    source = \"cshapes\",\n    20→    common_name = .add_years_in_name(common_name, start_year, end_year)\n    21→  )\n    22→\n    23→cshapes_common_names <- cshapes |>\n    24→  dplyr::select(original_name, source, common_name)\n    25→\n    26→k_polity_common_names |>\n    27→  dplyr::bind_rows(cshapes_common_names) |>\n    28→  readr::write_csv(\n    29→    fs::path(polities_inputs_path, \"common_names.csv\")\n    30→  )\n    31→\n    32→cshapes_polity_codes <- cshapes |>\n    33→  dplyr::anti_join(\n    34→    k_polity_codes,\n    35→    by = dplyr::join_by(common_name == polity_name)\n    36→  ) |>\n    37→  dplyr::mutate(\n    38→    polity_code = .build_auto_polity_code(polity_name, start_year, end_year)\n    39→  ) |>\n    40→  dplyr::distinct(polity_name = common_name, polity_code)\n    41→\n    42→k_polity_codes |>\n    43→  dplyr::bind_rows(cshapes_polity_codes) |>\n    44→  dplyr::arrange(polity_name, polity_code) |>\n    45→  readr::write_csv(\n    46→    fs::path(polities_inputs_path, \"polity_codes.csv\")\n    47→  )\n    48→"}]},"uuid":"bd885c96-cbe1-4655-a682-54c55c2801c1","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:27.766Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"996c06bb-00ff-457f-97ac-a09171910bc0","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

{"parentUuid":"c6c923d7-58fd-41f9-9725-80ca0ba69c66","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"progress","data":{"type":"hook_progress","hookEvent":"PostToolUse","hookName":"PostToolUse:Read","command":"callback"},"parentToolUseID":"toolu_01Qiz9nbzT9DBgnoHDcduCcd","toolUseID":"toolu_01Qiz9nbzT9DBgnoHDcduCcd","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:29.131Z","uuid":"28ce8dad-b10f-45eb-968c-63cffa2b4b6b","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"c2ba32a8-f62d-436f-a97a-904c6e934856","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01Qiz9nbzT9DBgnoHDcduCcd","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Polities Geometry Gap Analysis\n     2→\n     3→**Date**: 2026-03-24\n     4→\n     5→## The Problem\n     6→\n     7→The WHEP polities database needs polygon geometries for all 601 polities. Currently,\n     8→only CShapes 2.0 provides geometry, covering 288 polities. **195 non-region polities\n     9→lack geometry.** (118 are aggregate regions that may not need polygons.)\n    10→\n    11→## Current Geometry Coverage\n    12→\n    13→| Category | Count | % |\n    14→|----------|-------|---|\n    15→| **Have CShapes geometry** | 288 | 47.9% |\n    16→| No geometry (non-region) | 195 | 32.4% |\n    17→| Aggregate regions (no polygon expected) | 118 | 19.6% |\n    18→| **Total** | **601** | 100% |\n    19→\n    20→## Strategy for Filling Geometry Gaps\n    21→\n    22→The 195 missing polities can be addressed through 4 strategies:\n    23→\n    24→### Strategy 1: GADM / Natural Earth (96 polities)\n    25→\n    26→**Source**: GADM v4.1 (https://gadm.org/) or Natural Earth 1:10m\n    27→(https://www.naturalearthdata.com/)\n    28→\n    29→**What**: These are modern territories that still exist today (small islands, overseas\n    30→territories, microstates). Their boundaries haven't changed significantly, so current\n    31→polygons are adequate.\n    32→\n    33→**Format**: Shapefiles, GeoJSON, GeoPackage (free download)\n    34→\n    35→**Examples**:\n    36→- Andorra, Liechtenstein, Monaco, San Marino, Holy See\n    37→- Bermuda, Cayman Islands, British Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa\n    38→- Cook Islands, Niue, Tokelau, Pitcairn\n    39→- Aruba, Curaçao, Sint Maarten, Bonaire/St.Eustatius/Saba\n    40→- Åland Islands, Guernsey, Jersey, Isle of Man\n    41→- French Guiana, French Polynesia, Martinique, Guadeloupe, Réunion, Mayotte\n    42→- Christmas Island, Cocos Islands, Norfolk Island, etc.\n    43→- Greenland, Svalbard, Faroe Islands\n    44→- Hong Kong, Macao, Puerto Rico, US Virgin Islands\n    45→- Palestine, Western Sahara\n    46→- Seychelles, Nauru, Tuvalu, Tonga, Samoa, Palau, Marshall Islands\n    47→\n    48→**Effort**: LOW. Download GADM level 0 for each territory. Can be scripted.\n    49→\n    50→**GADM coverage**: GADM has separate entries for virtually ALL of these territories\n    51→at level 0. Natural Earth's `ne_10m_admin_0_countries` layer also includes most of\n    52→them, plus disputed areas and dependent territories.\n    53→\n    54→**Key tool**: The `rnaturalearth` R package can pull these directly:\n    55→```r\n    56→rnaturalearth::ne_countries(scale = 10, type = \"countries\")\n    57→rnaturalearth::ne_countries(scale = 10, type = \"sovereignty\") # includes dependents\n    58→```\n    59→\n    60→### Strategy 2: Combine Modern Country Borders (30 polities)\n    61→\n    62→**Source**: GADM level 0 (country borders) or level 1 (admin subdivisions)\n    63→\n    64→**What**: Colonial territories whose borders roughly match combinations of modern\n    65→countries or subnational units. The colonial territory polygon can be approximated\n    66→by dissolving/merging modern boundaries.\n    67→\n    68→**Examples**:\n    69→\n    70→| Colonial Polity | Modern Proxy |\n    71→|----------------|-------------|\n    72→| Belgian Congo | DRC (GADM level 0) |\n    73→| British East Africa | Kenya + Uganda |\n    74→| French West Africa | Senegal + Mali + Niger + Guinea + Côte d'Ivoire + Burkina Faso + Benin + Mauritania |\n    75→| French Equatorial Africa | Gabon + Congo + CAR + Chad |\n    76→| German East Africa (Tanganyika) | Tanzania mainland |\n    77→| Sudan (Anglo-Egyptian) | Sudan + South Sudan |\n    78→| Syria and Lebanon | Syria + Lebanon |\n    79→| Palestine/Jordan | Palestine + Jordan |\n    80→| Rwanda and Burundi | Rwanda + Burundi |\n    81→| Nyasaland Protectorate | Malawi |\n    82→| Basutoland | Lesotho |\n    83→| Bechuanaland Protectorate | Botswana |\n    84→| Gold Coast | Ghana |\n    85→| British Malaya | Peninsular Malaysia (GADM level 1) |\n    86→| Newfoundland | Newfoundland province (GADM Canada level 1) |\n    87→| Sarawak | Sarawak state (GADM Malaysia level 1) |\n    88→| Sabah (British Borneo) | Sabah state (GADM Malaysia level 1) |\n    89→| Manchukuo | Heilongjiang+Jilin+Liaoning+Inner Mongolia east (GADM China level 1) |\n    90→| Dutch East Indies | Indonesia |\n    91→| Dodecanese Islands | South Aegean region (GADM Greece level 2) |\n    92→| Italian Libia Cyrenaica | Libya |\n    93→| Spanish Guinea | Equatorial Guinea |\n    94→| New Hebrides | Vanuatu |\n    95→| British Protectorate (Somaliland) | Somaliland region of Somalia |\n    96→| Cameroon (Kamerun) | Cameroon (roughly) |\n    97→\n    98→**Effort**: MEDIUM. Requires selecting the right GADM units, merging polygons in R/sf.\n    99→Some colonial borders don't perfectly match modern borders (e.g., German Kamerun\n   100→included parts of modern Chad/Congo/Nigeria that differ from modern Cameroon).\n   101→\n   102→**Accuracy caveat**: Colonial boundaries were often different from modern boundaries.\n   103→This approach gives ~80-95% accuracy depending on the territory. For higher accuracy,\n   104→use the Aourednik Historical Basemaps or MPIDR Census Mosaic.\n   105→\n   106→### Strategy 3: Historical GIS Data (25 polities)\n   107→\n   108→**Source**: Multiple historical GIS datasets needed\n   109→\n   110→**What**: These are polities that existed before 1886 (pre-CShapes) and whose\n   111→territory doesn't correspond to any modern entity. They need actual historical\n   112→boundary data.\n   113→\n   114→**Polities requiring historical GIS**:\n   115→\n   116→**Pre-unification Italian states** (ended 1860-1870):\n   117→- Papal States (PAP-1800-1870): ~41,000 km2 (central Italy)\n   118→- Two Sicilies (TWO-1800-1860): ~111,900 km2 (southern Italy + Sicily)\n   119→- Sardinia (SAR-1800-1860): ~74,000 km2 (NW Italy + Sardinia island)\n   120→- Tuscany (TUS-1800-1860): ~22,000 km2 (central Italy)\n   121→- Duchy Modena (DMO-1800-1860): ~6,400 km2\n   122→- Duchy Parma (DPA-1800-1860): ~6,200 km2\n   123→\n   124→**Best source for Italian states**:\n   125→- Aourednik Historical Basemaps (1800 and 1850 snapshots have pre-unification states)\n   126→- Euratlas GIS (1800 and 1900 snapshots, paid)\n   127→- The MPIDR Census Mosaic may have Italian admin boundaries\n   128→\n   129→**Pre-unification/pre-annexation Canadian provinces** (ended 1866):\n   130→- Lower Quebec, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Ontario, Prince Edward Island,\n   131→  Vancouver's Island, British Columbia\n   132→**Source**: GADM Canada level 1 (modern provinces) is close enough\n   133→\n   134→**Central Asian khanates** (ended 1873-1920):\n   135→- Badakhshan (1800-1873), Khiva (1800-1873), Kokand (1800-1876), Bukhara (1800-1920)\n   136→**Source**: Aourednik 1850/1880 basemaps may include these\n   137→\n   138→**Other historical entities**:\n   139→- Ionian Islands (1815-1864): Use GADM Greece level 2 for Ionian Islands region\n   140→- Herat (1800-1862): Historical Afghan principality, ~70,000 km2\n   141→- Yunnan (1856-1871): Modern Yunnan province, GADM China level 1\n   142→- Danish India (1800-1845): Tranquebar + Serampore, very small areas\n   143→- Bonin Islands (1846-1873): Modern Ogasawara, GADM Japan level 2\n   144→- St Martin/St Barthélemy (1816-1878): Modern island polygons from Natural Earth\n   145→\n   146→**Effort**: HIGH for Italian states and Central Asian khanates. MEDIUM for others\n   147→(can use modern subnational boundaries as proxies).\n   148→\n   149→### Strategy 4: Other Sources for CShapes-Range Dependents (44 polities)\n   150→\n   151→**Source**: Natural Earth, GADM, Aourednik, or custom creation\n   152→\n   153→**What**: These polities existed within the CShapes coverage period (1886-2019) but\n   154→CShapes doesn't include them because they were dependent territories, not sovereign states.\n   155→\n   156→**Key subcategories**:\n   157→\n   158→**Gulf Emirates (pre-UAE, pre-1971)**:\n   159→- Abu Dhabi, Ajman, Fujairah, Ras al Khaimah, Sharjah, Umm al Qawain\n   160→**Source**: GADM UAE level 1 gives modern emirate boundaries. These haven't changed\n   161→much since the Trucial States period.\n   162→\n   163→**Small colonial concessions**:\n   164→- Kiautschou (German China lease, 1898-1922): ~552 km2 around Qingdao\n   165→- Kwang-Chou-Wan (French China lease): ~1,300 km2\n   166→- Kwantung/Port Arthur (Japanese lease): ~3,500 km2\n   167→**Source**: Would need manual GIS creation or historical maps\n   168→\n   169→**Pacific territories**:\n   170→- Gilbert and Ellice Islands, Caroline Islands, etc.\n   171→**Source**: Natural Earth or GADM for the modern island groups\n   172→\n   173→**African sub-territories**:\n   174→- British Bechuanaland (1885-1895): Northern Cape Province of South Africa (GADM)\n   175→- Gold Coast dependencies\n   176→\n   177→**Other**:\n   178→- Crete (1898-1913): GADM Greece level 2 (Crete region)\n   179→- Manchukuo (1932-1945): Already covered in Strategy 2\n   180→- Netherlands Antilles: GADM for individual islands\n   181→\n   182→**Effort**: MEDIUM. Most can be sourced from GADM subnational data.\n   183→\n   184→## Available Polygon Data Sources (Detailed)\n   185→\n   186→### Primary Sources (Free, Ready-to-Use)\n   187→\n   188→| Source | URL | Format | Coverage | Territories? |\n   189→|--------|-----|--------|----------|-------------|\n   190→| **CShapes 2.0** | icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/ | Shapefile, R pkg | 1886-2019, sovereign + some dependent | YES (some) |\n   191→| **GADM 4.1** | gadm.org | Shapefile, GeoJSON, GeoPackage | Current, all countries, L0-L5 | YES (all) |\n   192→| **Natural Earth 10m** | naturalearthdata.com | Shapefile | Current, world | YES (dependents, disputed) |\n   193→| **Aourednik** | github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps | GeoJSON | 54 snapshots, BCE-2010 | YES (empires, colonies) |\n   194→| **rnaturalearth** R pkg | CRAN | sf objects | Current | YES |\n   195→\n   196→### Secondary Sources (Free, Require Processing)\n   197→\n   198→| Source | URL | Coverage | Notes |\n   199→|--------|-----|----------|-------|\n   200→| **MPIDR Census Mosaic** | mosaic.ipums.org | Europe 1837-2003 | Registration required |\n   201→| **COW Territorial Change v6** | correlatesofwar.org | 1816-2018 (tabular) | No polygons, but areas |\n   202→| **Cliopatria** | cliopatria.io | All history | Areas only, no polygons |\n   203→| **Thenmap API** | thenmap.net | 1945-present | REST API, GeoJSON |\n   204→| **IPUMS IHGIS** | ihgis.ipums.org | Various census dates | Boundary shapefiles |\n   205→| **Wimmer Empire-to-Nation** | awimmer.com/data | 1816-2001 (tabular) | No polygons |\n   206→\n   207→### Historical Map Sources (Images for Georeferencing)\n   208→\n   209→| Source | URL | Content |\n   210→|--------|-----|---------|\n   211→| **David Rumsey Map Collection** | davidrumsey.com | 150,000+ historical maps, many georeferenced |\n   212→| **Old Maps Online** | oldmapsonline.org | Aggregator of many map collections |\n   213→| **Library of Congress** | loc.gov/maps/ | Extensive historical map collection |\n   214→| **British Library Maps** | bl.uk/collection-guides/maps | Colonial-era maps |\n   215→| **Gallica (BnF)** | gallica.bnf.fr | French colonial maps |\n   216→\n   217→## Recommended Implementation Plan\n   218→\n   219→### Phase 1: Quick Wins (96 polities, ~1 week effort)\n   220→Use GADM/Natural Earth for all modern small territories.\n   221→- Download GADM level 0 for each territory\n   222→- Match to polity codes via ISO3 codes\n   223→- Script in R using `geodata::gadm()` or `rnaturalearth`\n   224→\n   225→### Phase 2: Colonial Proxies (30 polities, ~1-2 weeks effort)\n   226→Combine modern country/subnational borders for colonial territories.\n   227→- Build a mapping table: colonial polity -> list of modern GADM units\n   228→- Dissolve boundaries in R/sf\n   229→- Manual verification against historical maps\n   230→\n   231→### Phase 3: CShapes-Range Dependents (44 polities, ~2-3 weeks effort)\n   232→Use GADM subnational data + Natural Earth for dependent territories.\n   233→- Gulf emirates from GADM UAE level 1\n   234→- Pacific islands from Natural Earth\n   235→- Historical concessions (Kiautschou, etc.) may need manual creation\n   236→\n   237→### Phase 4: Historical States (25 polities, ~3-4 weeks effort)\n   238→Use Aourednik Historical Basemaps + Euratlas + custom GIS.\n   239→- Italian pre-unification states from Aourednik 1850 snapshot\n   240→- Central Asian khanates from Aourednik 1850/1880\n   241→- Canadian provinces from GADM Canada level 1\n   242→- Remaining entities: manual creation from historical maps\n   243→\n   244→### Phase 5: Regions (118 polities, optional)\n   245→Aggregate regions (Africa, Asia, EU, etc.) can be built by dissolving\n   246→member country polygons using M49/FAOSTAT grouping definitions.\n   247→\n   248→## Total Effort Estimate\n   249→\n   250→| Phase | Polities | Effort | Source |\n   251→|-------|----------|--------|--------|\n   252→| Phase 1 | 96 | LOW (scriptable) | GADM + Natural Earth |\n   253→| Phase 2 | 30 | MEDIUM | GADM merge/dissolve |\n   254→| Phase 3 | 44 | MEDIUM-HIGH | GADM L1/L2 + manual |\n   255→| Phase 4 | 25 | HIGH | Aourednik + manual GIS |\n   256→| Phase 5 | 118 | LOW (scriptable) | Dissolve member countries |\n   257→| **Total** | **313** | **~8-10 weeks** | Multiple sources |\n   258→\n   259→With all phases complete, **100% of polities would have geometry**.\n   260→"}]},"uuid":"8ea6da82-56f2-41e0-a4e2-ecfc78f47fcd","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:29.130Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"c2ba32a8-f62d-436f-a97a-904c6e934856","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"8ea6da82-56f2-41e0-a4e2-ecfc78f47fcd","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"progress","data":{"type":"hook_progress","hookEvent":"PostToolUse","hookName":"PostToolUse:Read","command":"callback"},"parentToolUseID":"toolu_01PUngUKgYAu3K8Mj1rarxbB","toolUseID":"toolu_01PUngUKgYAu3K8Mj1rarxbB","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:29.155Z","uuid":"bcaf73a3-b8a0-4608-8cff-76e1d2016bba","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"40b741d0-3a6e-40a3-9b02-8609cfc62efe","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01PUngUKgYAu3K8Mj1rarxbB","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→#' WHEP polities\n     2→#'\n     3→#' @description\n     4→#' Lists all polities defined in the WHEP project that are obtained merging\n     5→#' together information from several sources.\n     6→#' The term polity comes from _political entity_ and is a more general term\n     7→#' than country.\n     8→#'\n     9→#' A polity can be defined as a fixed territory over a continuous\n    10→#' period of time. Whenever a region changes its overall territory, it should\n    11→#' start being treated as a new polity. Likewise, if there's only a change in\n    12→#' official name for a country or region, but no territory change, it will\n    13→#' still be the same polity.\n    14→#'\n    15→#' In most cases a polity is included in this list as a consequence of it\n    16→#' having reported production or trade data individually, as opposed to this\n    17→#' data being included in a larger territory. In this sense, most colonies are\n    18→#' considered their own polities if there is data for them.\n    19→#'\n    20→#' @returns\n    21→#' An sf object on top of a tibble where each row represents one polity.\n    22→#' It has the following columns:\n    23→#' - `polity_name`: Natural language name that uniquely represents a polity.\n    24→#'   It must not necessarily match the official name of the polity at some\n    25→#'   point in time. As a consequence, some names have been shortened or adapted\n    26→#'   for easier understanding. Polity names might change in the future as\n    27→#'   long as they are still unique. If you need to perform checks for specific\n    28→#'   polities, use `polity_code` instead.\n    29→#' - `polity_code`: Internal code that uniquely represents each polity. It\n    30→#'    follows a specific format for easier understanding. The format is\n    31→#'    `\"XXX-yyyy-YYYY\"`, where:\n    32→#'    - `XXX` is a corresponding ISO3 code if one exists, or an artificial one\n    33→#'      otherwise, but trying to be meaningful if possible.\n    34→#'    - `yyyy` is the `start_year` of the polity.\n    35→#'    - `YYYY` is the `end_year` of the polity.\n    36→#'\n    37→#'    For polities that traditionally refer to the same country (just a\n    38→#'    territory change), the `XXX` part will typically be the same as a hint,\n    39→#'    so that only the year range will vary.\n    40→#' - `start_year`: The year in which the polity starts.\n    41→#' - `end_year`: The year in which the polity ends.\n    42→#' - `m49_code`: The M49 code for the polity if one exists, as defined by the\n    43→#'   [United Nations](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/overview/).\n    44→#' - `iso3_code`: One valid\n    45→#'   [ISO 3166-1 alpha-3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-3)\n    46→#'   code, if such code exists. Note: One polity could have more than one such\n    47→#'   code. If this situation is found, this column might be moved to a different\n    48→#'   table.\n    49→#' - `iso2_code`: One valid\n    50→#'   [ISO 3166-1 alpha-2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2)\n    51→#'   code, if such code exists. Note: One polity could have more than one such\n    52→#'   code. If this situation is found, this column might be moved to a different\n    53→#'   table.\n    54→#' - `display_code`: A short code that uniquely represents a polity and might be\n    55→#'   useful for visualization purposes. It's initially intended to have the\n    56→#'   value as `polity_code`, but could change if necessary, as opposed to the\n    57→#'   `polity_code` not changing.\n    58→#' - `geometry`: An sf `MULTIPOLYGON` with the territorial information for the\n    59→#'   polity. Ideally each polity should have a non empty geometry, but this\n    60→#'   might not hold while the table is a work in progress.\n    61→#'\n    62→#' @details\n    63→#' This dataset itself was built from various other datasets. The methodology\n    64→#' followed to get the final result is roughly like this:\n    65→#'\n    66→#' 1. Clean data coming from other sources and prepare a tibble with\n    67→#'   same columns for all of them, so that they're all homogeneous. Check\n    68→#'   `get_polity_sources()` to know more about each source.\n    69→#' 2. Put together in a single tibble all rows from the different sources that\n    70→#'   were prepared in the previous step.\n    71→#' 3. Assign a polity name for each entry in the combined tibble. This name is\n    72→#'   also referred to as a _common name_, in the sense that a polity could come\n    73→#'   from more than one source, each having different names, and we want a\n    74→#'   common one to be able to group these into a single entry. This step is\n    75→#'   performed using a join with a manually crafted set of mappings from a\n    76→#'   combination of both original name and source, to a common name.\n    77→#' 4. Aggregate specific source data into a single entry, grouping by the common\n    78→#'   name defined in previous step. For now, this common name will also be the\n    79→#'   final value of `polity_name`. The aggregations are these:\n    80→#'     - `start_year` and `end_year`: A simple minimum and maximum is calculated\n    81→#'     for `start_year` and `end_year` respectively, with two edge cases:\n    82→#'       - If an entry in the `whep` source is present for this polity, use its\n    83→#'         year range directly. This is a simple way of overwriting the year\n    84→#'         range in cases where the original years needed to be changed for some\n    85→#'         reason, and the min/max approach does not give the desired year.\n    86→#'       - If all values are `NA` for either `start_year` or `end_year`, a\n    87→#'         default value is used instead, which denotes the limits of the period\n    88→#'         we want to cover. These years are 1800 (earliest source used) and\n    89→#'         2025 (present day) respectively. This also applies for the edge case\n    90→#'         of using the `whep` source. If there is an `NA` value there, the\n    91→#'         default value is used.\n    92→#'     - `m49_code`, `iso2_code`, `iso3_code` and `geometry`: Check whether\n    93→#'       there is a single unique value, excluding `NA`s, and use that value as\n    94→#'       aggregate. Otherwise, fail. This can change in the future if there is\n    95→#'       something meaningful to merge but for now it's a useful way to make\n    96→#'       sure there is no information loss.\n    97→#' 5. Assign a unique code (`polity_code`) for each polity. This is also done\n    98→#'   with a manually crafted mapping, which is used to perform a join. The\n    99→#'   mapping table defines the polity code for each polity name, the common\n   100→#'   name created before, which also uniquely defines a polity. However, after\n   101→#'   this code is set, all subsequent operations on polities should be done\n   102→#'   using this code instead of the polity name, since it is expected for the\n   103→#'   code not to change in the future, but the polity name could, so it's less\n   104→#'   reliable.\n   105→#' 6. Create the `display_code`. As mentioned before, this is currently just\n   106→#'   the same as `polity_code`, but could change in the future if it seems a\n   107→#'   good idea to use other intuitive short names.\n   108→#'\n   109→#' @export\n   110→#'\n   111→#' @examples\n   112→#' get_polities()\n   113→get_polities <- function() {\n   114→  .merge_datasets() |>\n   115→    .add_common_names() |>\n   116→    .aggregate_cols() |>\n   117→    .set_polity_name_code() |>\n   118→    .build_display_code() |>\n   119→    .set_column_types() |>\n   120→    dplyr::arrange(polity_name) |>\n   121→    sf::st_as_sf() |>\n   122→    dplyr::select(\n   123→      polity_name,\n   124→      polity_code,\n   125→      start_year,\n   126→      end_year,\n   127→      m49_code,\n   128→      iso3_code,\n   129→      iso2_code,\n   130→      display_code\n   131→    )\n   132→}\n   133→\n   134→#' WHEP polity sources\n   135→#'\n   136→#' @description\n   137→#' Lists all sources for each requested polity along with their specific data.\n   138→#'\n   139→#' @param polity_codes The values of `polity_code` for the requested polities.\n   140→#'   This argument accepts three different kinds of values:\n   141→#'   - A single string, the `polity_code` of the single requested polity.\n   142→#'   - A character vector, containing all the values of `polity_code` for the\n   143→#'     requested polities.\n   144→#'   - Default `NULL` value, which is equivalent to passing a character vector\n   145→#'     containing the codes of all polities present in `get_polities()`.\n   146→#'\n   147→#' @returns\n   148→#' A single tibble with the following columns:\n   149→#' - `original_name`: The natural language name for the region in that specific\n   150→#'   source, before defining a common name.\n   151→#' - `source`: The source this region was taken from. It can be one of the\n   152→#'   following:\n   153→#'   - `federico_tena`: [Federico-Tena list of polities\n   154→#'     ](https://edatos.consorciomadrono.es/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21950/3SK54X),\n   155→#'     covering the period 1800-1938. This is the earliest source used, being\n   156→#'     the only one that covers the whole nineteenth century. It includes no\n   157→#'     geometry.\n   158→#'   - `faostat`: [FAOSTAT country/region list\n   159→#'     ](https://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#definitions) that is used in their\n   160→#'     agricultural production and trade data, and covers from 1961 to\n   161→#'     present day. It includes no geometry.\n   162→#'   - `m49`: [United Nations list of countries\n   163→#'     ](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/overview/) using the M49\n   164→#'     standard. It was first issued in 1970 and aims to cover present day\n   165→#'     countries, but here some historical codes no longer in use are also\n   166→#'     included. It includes no geometry.\n   167→#'   - `cshapes`: [CShapes 2.0](https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/) mapping\n   168→#'     of territorial changes in independent and dependent states. It covers\n   169→#'     the period 1886-2017. For now this is the only source that includes\n   170→#'     the geometry of the polity's territory.\n   171→#'   - `whep`: Custom made source for overwriting specific data. For now,\n   172→#'     it is only used to overwrite the year range for a polity when the\n   173→#'     one calculated automatically is not satisfying. Thus, it includes\n   174→#'     no geometry or other data, but this could change in the future if\n   175→#'     it becomes a necessity.\n   176→#' - `common_name`: The given name that will be shared by entries across\n   177→#'   sources if they represent the same polity. This will be `polity_name`\n   178→#'   in the main tibble obtained from `get_polities()`.\n   179→#' - `polity_code`: The unique code identifying the polity. Note that this\n   180→#'   is included as a helper but there might be more than one row with the\n   181→#'   same `polity_code` in this table if the polity appears in more than\n   182→#'   one source.\n   183→#' - `notes`: A natural language description of the polity, focused on\n   184→#'   clarifying the existence of this entry, possibly including its relation\n   185→#'   with other entries. In the case of `whep` source entries, it explains\n   186→#'   why that entry was created to overwrite the other sources' information.\n   187→#' - Other information specifically found in that source that might be\n   188→#'   aggregated in the final polity table. This includes `start_year`,\n   189→#'   `end_year`, `m49_code`, `iso2_code`, `iso3_code` and `geometry`. For\n   190→#'   more details about these, check `get_polities()`.\n   191→#'\n   192→#' @export\n   193→#'\n   194→#' @examples\n   195→#' get_polity_sources()\n   196→#' get_polity_sources(\"VEN-1821-2025\")\n   197→#' get_polity_sources(c(\"VEN-1821-2025\", \"UGA-1962-2025\"))\n   198→get_polity_sources <- function(polity_codes = NULL) {\n   199→  polities <- get_polities() |>\n   200→    tibble::as_tibble()\n   201→\n   202→  if (is.null(polity_codes)) {\n   203→    polity_codes <- polities |>\n   204→      dplyr::select(polity_code)\n   205→  } else {\n   206→    polity_codes <- tibble::tibble(polity_code = polity_codes)\n   207→  }\n   208→\n   209→  polity_names <- polities |>\n   210→    dplyr::inner_join(polity_codes, by = \"polity_code\") |>\n   211→    dplyr::select(common_name = polity_name, polity_code)\n   212→\n   213→  .merge_datasets() |>\n   214→    .add_common_names() |>\n   215→    dplyr::inner_join(polity_names, by = \"common_name\") |>\n   216→    dplyr::arrange(original_name)\n   217→}\n   218→\n   219→.merge_datasets <- function() {\n   220→  dplyr::bind_rows(\n   221→    .prepare_historical_m49(),\n   222→    .prepare_faostat(),\n   223→    .prepare_federico_tena(),\n   224→    .prepare_cshapes(),\n   225→    .prepare_whep_fixes()\n   226→  )\n   227→}\n   228→\n   229→.add_common_names <- function(merged_datasets) {\n   230→  merged_datasets |>\n   231→    dplyr::inner_join(\n   232→      k_polity_common_names,\n   233→      by = c(\"original_name\", \"source\"),\n   234→      unmatched = \"error\",\n   235→    )\n   236→}\n   237→\n   238→.set_polity_name_code <- function(merged_datasets) {\n   239→  merged_datasets |>\n   240→    dplyr::rename(polity_name = common_name) |>\n   241→    dplyr::inner_join(\n   242→      k_polity_codes,\n   243→      by = \"polity_name\",\n   244→      unmatched = \"error\"\n   245→    )\n   246→}\n   247→\n   248→.set_column_types <- function(polities) {\n   249→  int_cols <- c(\"start_year\", \"end_year\")\n   250→  geom_cols <- c(\"geometry\")\n   251→  chr_cols <- setdiff(names(polities), c(int_cols, geom_cols))\n   252→\n   253→  polities |>\n   254→    dplyr::mutate(\n   255→      across(all_of(int_cols), as.integer),\n   256→      across(all_of(chr_cols), as.character)\n   257→    )\n   258→}\n   259→\n   260→.prepare_historical_m49 <- function() {\n   261→  k_historical_m49 |>\n   262→    # TODO: Do this cleanly. M49 code 728 is reused, so only use for new country\n   263→    dplyr::mutate(\n   264→      m49_code = ifelse(m49_name == \"Spanish North Africa\", NA, m49_code)\n   265→    ) |>\n   266→    dplyr::mutate(source = k_source_m49) |>\n   267→    dplyr::select(\n   268→      original_name = m49_name,\n   269→      source,\n   270→      start_year,\n   271→      end_year,\n   272→      notes,\n   273→      m49_code\n   274→    )\n   275→}\n   276→\n   277→.prepare_faostat <- function() {\n   278→  k_faostat_regions |>\n   279→    dplyr::mutate(source = k_source_faostat) |>\n   280→    dplyr::select(\n   281→      original_name = country_name,\n   282→      source,\n   283→      start_year,\n   284→      end_year,\n   285→      m49_code,\n   286→      iso2_code,\n   287→      iso3_code\n   288→    )\n   289→}\n   290→\n   291→.prepare_federico_tena <- function() {\n   292→  k_federico_tena_polities |>\n   293→    dplyr::mutate(\n   294→      source = k_source_federico_tena,\n   295→      # Force first year because federico tena has earliest cover\n   296→      start_year = ifelse(is.na(start_year), k_polity_first_year, start_year)\n   297→    ) |>\n   298→    dplyr::select(\n   299→      original_name = polity_name,\n   300→      source,\n   301→      start_year,\n   302→      end_year,\n   303→      notes\n   304→    )\n   305→}\n   306→\n   307→.prepare_cshapes <- function() {\n   308→  k_cshapes |>\n   309→    dplyr::mutate(\n   310→      source = k_source_cshapes,\n   311→      polity_name = .add_years_in_name(polity_name, start_year, end_year)\n   312→    ) |>\n   313→    dplyr::select(\n   314→      original_name = polity_name,\n   315→      source,\n   316→      start_year,\n   317→      end_year,\n   318→      geometry\n   319→    )\n   320→}\n   321→\n   322→.prepare_whep_fixes <- function() {\n   323→  k_whep_polity_fixes |>\n   324→    dplyr::mutate(source = k_source_whep) |>\n   325→    dplyr::select(\n   326→      original_name = polity_name,\n   327→      source,\n   328→      start_year,\n   329→      end_year,\n   330→      notes\n   331→    )\n   332→}\n   333→\n   334→# TODO: Think if worth to shorten, maybe another mapping table\n   335→.build_display_code <- function(polities) {\n   336→  polities |>\n   337→    dplyr::mutate(display_code = polity_code)\n   338→}\n   339→\n   340→.aggregate_cols <- function(polities) {\n   341→  unique_cols <- c(\"m49_code\", \"iso2_code\", \"iso3_code\")\n   342→\n   343→  polities |>\n   344→    dplyr::summarise(\n   345→      start_year = .aggregate_start_year(start_year, source),\n   346→      end_year = .aggregate_end_year(end_year, source),\n   347→      across(all_of(unique_cols), .check_unique_value),\n   348→      geometry = .check_unique_geometry(geometry),\n   349→      .by = \"common_name\"\n   350→    )\n   351→}\n   352→\n   353→.aggregate_start_year <- function(start_year, source) {\n   354→  # Force year from whep source if present\n   355→  i <- purrr::detect_index(source, ~ .x == k_source_whep)\n   356→\n   357→  if (i > 0 && is.na(start_year[i])) {\n   358→    k_polity_first_year\n   359→  } else if (i > 0) {\n   360→    start_year[i]\n   361→  } else if (all(is.na(start_year))) {\n   362→    k_polity_first_year\n   363→  } else {\n   364→    min(start_year, na.rm = TRUE)\n   365→  }\n   366→}\n   367→\n   368→.aggregate_end_year <- function(end_year, source) {\n   369→  i <- purrr::detect_index(source, ~ .x == k_source_whep)\n   370→\n   371→  if (i > 0 && is.na(end_year[i])) {\n   372→    k_polity_last_year\n   373→  } else if (i > 0) {\n   374→    end_year[i]\n   375→  } else if (all(is.na(end_year))) {\n   376→    k_polity_last_year\n   377→  } else {\n   378→    max(end_year, na.rm = TRUE)\n   379→  }\n   380→}\n   381→\n   382→.check_unique_geometry <- function(column) {\n   383→  unique_vals <- column |>\n   384→    purrr::discard(sf::st_is_empty) |>\n   385→    unique()\n   386→\n   387→  if (length(unique_vals) > 1) {\n   388→    cli::cli_abort(\"Can't summarise, more than one unique value: {unique_vals}\")\n   389→  } else if (length(unique_vals) == 0) {\n   390→    sf::st_sfc(sf::st_multipolygon(), crs = sf::st_crs(column))\n   391→  } else {\n   392→    sf::st_sfc(unique_vals, crs = sf::st_crs(column))\n   393→  }\n   394→}\n   395→\n   396→.check_unique_value <- function(column) {\n   397→  unique_vals <- column |>\n   398→    na.omit() |>\n   399→    unique()\n   400→\n   401→  if (length(unique_vals) > 1) {\n   402→    cli::cli_abort(\"Can't summarise, more than one unique value: {unique_vals}\")\n   403→  } else if (length(unique_vals) == 0) {\n   404→    NA\n   405→  } else {\n   406→    unique_vals\n   407→  }\n   408→}\n   409→\n   410→# Used for dataset generation in constants.R\n   411→.clean_faostat_regions <- function() {\n   412→  \"faostat_regions\" |>\n   413→    whep_read_file() |>\n   414→    dplyr::select(\n   415→      country_code = `Country Code`,\n   416→      country_name = Country,\n   417→      m49_code = `M49 Code`,\n   418→      iso2_code = `ISO2 Code`,\n   419→      iso3_code = `ISO3 Code`,\n   420→      start_year = `Start Year`,\n   421→      end_year = `End Year`\n   422→    )\n   423→}\n   424→\n   425→.clean_unstats_m49 <- function() {\n   426→  \"unstats_m49\" |>\n   427→    whep_read_file() |>\n   428→    dplyr::select(\n   429→      m49_code = `M49 Code`,\n   430→      m49_name = `Country or Area`,\n   431→      region1_code = `Intermediate Region Code`,\n   432→      region1_name = `Intermediate Region Name`,\n   433→      region2_code = `Sub-region Code`,\n   434→      region2_name = `Sub-region Name`,\n   435→      region3_code = `Region Code`,\n   436→      region3_name = `Region Name`,\n   437→      region4_code = `Global Code`,\n   438→      region4_name = `Global Name`,\n   439→      iso2_code = `ISO-alpha2 Code`,\n   440→      iso3_code = `ISO-alpha3 Code`,\n   441→      least_developed = `Least Developed Countries (LDC)`,\n   442→      land_locked_developing = `Land Locked Developing Countries (LLDC)`,\n   443→      small_island_developing = `Small Island Developing States (SIDS)`\n   444→    ) |>\n   445→    dplyr::mutate(\n   446→      across(\n   447→        c(least_developed, land_locked_developing, small_island_developing),\n   448→        ~ !is.na(.x)\n   449→      )\n   450→    )\n   451→}\n   452→\n   453→.clean_historical_m49 <- function() {\n   454→  \"historical_m49\" |>\n   455→    whep_read_file() |>\n   456→    dplyr::mutate(is_old = !is.na(end_year)) |>\n   457→    dplyr::select(-iso3166_code, -code_mismatch)\n   458→}\n   459→\n   460→.clean_federico_tena_polities <- function() {\n   461→  \"federico_tena_polities\" |>\n   462→    whep_read_file() |>\n   463→    dplyr::select(\n   464→      polity_name = list_of_trading_polities,\n   465→      start_year = trading_polity_starting,\n   466→      end_year = trading_polity_end,\n   467→      population = population_1913_000,\n   468→      notes = notes\n   469→    ) |>\n   470→    # There was an empty row in the original dataset\n   471→    dplyr::filter(!is.na(polity_name)) |>\n   472→    # Remove virtual time boundaries due to dataset study range\n   473→    dplyr::mutate(\n   474→      start_year = ifelse(start_year == 1800, NA, start_year),\n   475→      end_year = ifelse(end_year >= 1938, NA, end_year)\n   476→    )\n   477→}\n   478→\n   479→.clean_cshapes <- function() {\n   480→  .load_cshapes() |>\n   481→    .filter_relevant_area_changes() |>\n   482→    # Remove virtual time boundaries due to dataset study range\n   483→    dplyr::mutate(\n   484→      start_year = ifelse(start_year <= 1886, NA, start_year),\n   485→      end_year = ifelse(end_year >= 2019, NA, end_year)\n   486→    ) |>\n   487→    dplyr::select(\n   488→      polity_name = country_name,\n   489→      start_year,\n   490→      end_year,\n   491→      area\n   492→    ) |>\n   493→    dplyr::arrange(polity_name)\n   494→}\n   495→\n   496→.load_cshapes <- function() {\n   497→  countries <- cshapes::cshp() |>\n   498→    sf::st_make_valid()\n   499→\n   500→  countries |>\n   501→    dplyr::mutate(\n   502→      area = countries |>\n   503→        sf::st_area() |>\n   504→        units::set_units(km^2),\n   505→      start_year = lubridate::year(start),\n   506→      end_year = lubridate::year(end),\n   507→    )\n   508→}\n   509→\n   510→.filter_relevant_area_changes <- function(countries) {\n   511→  zero_km2 <- units::set_units(0, km^2)\n   512→\n   513→  countries |>\n   514→    dplyr::arrange(country_name, start_year) |>\n   515→    dplyr::group_by(country_name) |>\n   516→    # Remove same year changes\n   517→    dplyr::filter(dplyr::n() == 1 | start_year != end_year) |>\n   518→    # Aggregate adjacent entries with no area change\n   519→    dplyr::mutate(group_tmp = cumsum(c(0, diff(area) != zero_km2))) |>\n   520→    dplyr::ungroup() |>\n   521→    dplyr::summarise(\n   522→      geometry = dplyr::first(geometry),\n   523→      area = dplyr::first(area),\n   524→      start_year = min(start_year),\n   525→      end_year = max(end_year),\n   526→      .by = c(\"country_name\", \"group_tmp\")\n   527→    ) |>\n   528→    dplyr::select(-group_tmp)\n   529→}\n   530→\n   531→.add_years_in_name <- function(name, start_year, end_year) {\n   532→  dplyr::case_when(\n   533→    is.na(end_year) ~ stringr::str_glue(\"{name}\"),\n   534→    is.na(start_year) ~ stringr::str_glue(\"{name} (to {end_year})\"),\n   535→    .default = stringr::str_glue(\"{name} ({start_year}-{end_year})\"),\n   536→  )\n   537→}\n   538→\n   539→.build_auto_polity_code <- function(name, start_year, end_year) {\n   540→  start_year <- ifelse(is.na(start_year), k_polity_first_year, start_year)\n   541→  end_year <- ifelse(is.na(end_year), k_polity_last_year, end_year)\n   542→  short <- name |>\n   543→    stringr::str_to_upper() |>\n   544→    stringr::str_sub(1, 3)\n   545→\n   546→  stringr::str_glue(\"{short}-{start_year}-{end_year}\")\n   547→}\n   548→\n   549→# Use for debugging when you get failed test because of polity\n   550→# code not matching short name or year range.\n   551→.get_bad_code_polities <- function() {\n   552→  get_polities() |>\n   553→    tidyr::separate_wider_delim(\n   554→      polity_code,\n   555→      \"-\",\n   556→      names = c(\"code_iso\", \"code_start_year\", \"code_end_year\"),\n   557→      cols_remove = FALSE\n   558→    ) |>\n   559→    dplyr::filter(\n   560→      as.integer(code_start_year) != start_year |\n   561→        as.integer(code_end_year) != end_year\n   562→    ) |>\n   563→    dplyr::select(-code_iso, -code_start_year, -code_end_year)\n   564→}\n   565→\n   566→# TODO: todos from removed old code:\n   567→# - Revise Edu's polities for special handlings\n   568→# - Introduce 'rest of continent'-like polities\n   569→#   \"ROCE\" \"RAFR\" \"RASI\" \"REUR\" \"RLAM\" \"RNAM\"\n   570→"}]},"uuid":"3a7692bc-3c4d-42d2-ac51-a9338ad05278","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:29.154Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"40b741d0-3a6e-40a3-9b02-8609cfc62efe","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

{"parentUuid":"cf3624b7-8c51-40aa-bae0-e2583eb146d2","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01NAsDaRVffZFvsyYjBAQmY4","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"cshapes R package cshp() columns status field Gleditsch-Ward COW codes\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"cshapes source: R/cshp.R\",\"url\":\"https://rdrr.io/cran/cshapes/src/R/cshp.R\"},{\"title\":\"ETH - International Conflict Research - R Package for CShapes\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/r-package/\"},{\"title\":\"ETH - International Conflict Research - R Package for CShapes Examples\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/r-package/examples.html\"},{\"title\":\"cshp function - RDocumentation\",\"url\":\"https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/cshapes/versions/0.6/topics/cshp\"},{\"title\":\"ETH - International Conflict Research - CShapes\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/\"},{\"title\":\"CShapes Examples | nils weidmann\",\"url\":\"http://nils.weidmann.ws/projects/cshapes/r-package/examples.html\"},{\"title\":\"cshp function - Access the CShapes dataset in R\",\"url\":\"https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/cshapes/versions/2.0/topics/cshp\"},{\"title\":\"Package ‘cshapes’ July 22, 2025 Type Package\",\"url\":\"https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/cshapes/cshapes.pdf\"},{\"title\":\"cshapes-package function - RDocumentation\",\"url\":\"https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/cshapes/versions/0.6/topics/cshapes-package\"},{\"title\":\"Full article: The Geography of the International System: The CShapes Dataset\",\"url\":\"https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/03050620903554614\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, I found information about the cshapes R package and its relationship to Gleditsch-Ward and COW codes:\n\n## Key Findings:\n\n**System Membership Coding:**\nThe `cshp()` function has a `useGW` Boolean argument specifying the system membership coding: TRUE for Gleditsch and Ward (GW, default), and FALSE for Correlates of War (COW).\n\n**Data Structure:**\nThe `cshp()` function returns an sf dataframe containing the complete CShapes dataset, or the CShapes snapshot for the specified date.\n\n**Available Fields:**\nFrom the examples in the search results, the dataset includes fields such as:\n- `COWCODE`: The COW codes field in the cshapes dataset\n- `CAPLAT`: The latitude coordinates of the capital\n\n**Coding System Compatibility:**\nThe dataset is coded according to both the Correlates of War and the Gleditsch and Ward (1999) state lists.\n\nHowever, the search results don't provide detailed information about a specific \"status\" field. If you're looking for more details about specific column names and their definitions, you may want to check the official CShapes documentation or the package's documentation files directly.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"0b522bc0-219f-4955-9ece-18cf6f281652","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:31.594Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"cf3624b7-8c51-40aa-bae0-e2583eb146d2","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"3d4c83d5-2c47-49cb-be75-56a91cea15de","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_013zggvjdtuLCYKXQmUi5Sfj","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"Schvitz et al 2022 \"Mapping the International System\" CShapes 2.0 Journal of Conflict Resolution dependent territories sovereign states\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset - Guy Schvitz, Luc Girardin, Seraina Rüegger, Nils B. Weidmann, Lars-Erik Cederman, Kristian Skrede Gleditsch, 2022\",\"url\":\"https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/00220027211013563\"},{\"title\":\"ETH - International Conflict Research - CShapes\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019 : The CShapes 2.0 Dataset\",\"url\":\"https://kops.uni-konstanz.de/entities/publication/31a2030e-4322-4229-93b2-cd6e27e1be8d\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset\",\"url\":\"https://ideas.repec.org/a/sae/jocore/v66y2022i1p144-161.html\"},{\"title\":\"ETH - International Conflict Research - Mapping The International System, 1886-2017: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/publications/cshapes-2/\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset - Research Repository\",\"url\":\"https://repository.essex.ac.uk/31791/\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping The International System, 1886-2017: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset - Big Ten Academic Alliance Geoportal\",\"url\":\"https://geo.btaa.org/catalog/b23a624e-f1ab-4989-8c94-f99f6e6fa414\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset - vLex United States - vLex\",\"url\":\"https://law-journals-books.vlex.com/vid/mapping-the-international-system-879944959\"},{\"title\":\"[PDF] Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset | Semantic Scholar\",\"url\":\"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Mapping-the-International-System,-1886-2019:-The-Schvitz-Girardin/a13fa3285df251d88629539fe9a62ac85c37ea2d\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset | Request PDF\",\"url\":\"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/342342479_Mapping_the_International_System_1886-2019_The_CShapes_20_Dataset\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, I found comprehensive information about the paper you're looking for. Here's what I found:\n\n## Publication Details\n\nThe article introduces CShapes 2.0, a GIS dataset that maps the borders of states and dependent territories from 1886 through 2019. The article was published in Journal of Conflict Resolution in 2022, Volume 66, Issue 1, pages 144-161.\n\n## Key Features\n\nThe dataset makes two significant improvements over the previous CShapes version:\n\n1. **Temporal Extension**: It extends temporal coverage from 1946 back to the year 1886, which followed the Berlin Conference on the partition of Africa.\n\n2. **Coverage of Dependent Territories**: The new dataset is no longer limited to independent states, but also maps the borders of colonies and other dependencies, thereby providing near complete global coverage of political units throughout recent history.\n\n## Key Applications\n\nThe new data can be used to examine trends in state size and to produce spatial estimates of populations under colonial rule. The authors also examined the relationship between the age of a border and the risk of interstate conflict, finding that the likelihood of militarized conflict between neighboring states decreases the longer their borders have remained in place.\n\nThe full article is available at the Journal of Conflict Resolution (DOI: 10.1177/00220027211013563).\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"47fbd6c6-6efd-4a5a-89df-50bacd664355","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:32.050Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"3d4c83d5-2c47-49cb-be75-56a91cea15de","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"b68ee234-add0-4bb5-9f07-a327dc84687d","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01P92sD79o1iJD8fMFcW2DYh","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"GADM CRS projection EPSG Natural Earth CRS WGS84\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"EPSG Geodetic Parameter Dataset - Wikipedia\",\"url\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EPSG_Geodetic_Parameter_Dataset\"},{\"title\":\"WGS 84 - WGS84 - World Geodetic System 1984, used in GPS - EPSG:4326\",\"url\":\"https://epsg.io/4326\"},{\"title\":\"WGS 84 (geocentric) - EPSG:4328\",\"url\":\"https://epsg.io/4328\"},{\"title\":\"World Geodetic System - Wikipedia\",\"url\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Geodetic_System\"},{\"title\":\"Projections — GeoPandas 1.1.3+0.gf5fe3ff.dirty documentation\",\"url\":\"https://geopandas.org/en/stable/docs/user_guide/projections.html\"},{\"title\":\"OGR Coordinate Reference Systems and Coordinate Transformation tutorial — GDAL documentation\",\"url\":\"https://gdal.org/en/stable/tutorials/osr_api_tut.html\"},{\"title\":\"Overview of Coordinate Reference Systems (CRS) in R\",\"url\":\"https://www.nceas.ucsb.edu/sites/default/files/2020-04/OverviewCoordinateReferenceSystems.pdf\"},{\"title\":\"WGS 84 / Equal Earth Greenwich - EPSG:8857\",\"url\":\"https://epsg.io/8857\"},{\"title\":\"Understand EPSG, WKT and Other CRS Definition Styles | Earth Data Science - Earth Lab\",\"url\":\"https://earthdatascience.org/courses/use-data-open-source-python/intro-vector-data-python/spatial-data-vector-shapefiles/epsg-proj4-coordinate-reference-system-formats-python/\"},{\"title\":\"2 Coordinate Reference Systems – Spatial Analysis and Mapping with R: A Short Tutorial\",\"url\":\"https://ubalt.pressbooks.pub/spatialanalysisenvironmentalstatisticsr/chapter/coordinate-reference-systems/\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, here's information about CRS projections, EPSG codes, and WGS84:\n\n## Key CRS and EPSG Information\n\n**EPSG:4326 - WGS84 Geographic CRS**\nEPSG:4326 is the WGS 84 datum ensemble for 2D (latitude, longitude) coordinates with 2 meter accuracy, used by the Global Positioning System among others.\n\n**EPSG Overview**\nThe EPSG registry is a public registry of geodetic datums, spatial reference systems, Earth ellipsoids, coordinate transformations and related units of measurement, with each entity assigned an EPSG code between 1024 and 32767, along with a standard machine-readable well-known text (WKT) representation.\n\n**WGS84 Datum**\nThe World Geodetic System (WGS) is a standard used in cartography, geodesy, and satellite navigation including GPS. The current version, WGS 84, defines an Earth-centered, Earth-fixed coordinate system and a geodetic datum, and also describes the associated Earth Gravitational Model (EGM) and World Magnetic Model (WMM).\n\n## CRS Formats and Standards\n\nUsing the PROJ.4 syntax, you specify the complete set of parameters including the ellipse, datum, projection units and projection definition that define a particular CRS.\n\nGenerally, WKT or SRID's are preferred over PROJ strings as they can contain more information about a given CRS. Conversions between WKT and PROJ strings will in most cases cause a loss of information, potentially leading to erroneous transformations.\n\nThe search results provide comprehensive information about EPSG codes and CRS standards, though they don't specifically mention GADM or Natural Earth data CRS requirements.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"8d948fd0-f612-42da-88ef-fbe66127168b","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:34.372Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"b68ee234-add0-4bb5-9f07-a327dc84687d","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"d9dc846f-f0e0-475a-b789-1b48d2cd3a90","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01TubUj5tZwPi4TFxKHshKgH","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"Aourednik historical basemaps CRS projection coordinate reference system\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"GitHub - aourednik/historical-basemaps: Collection of georeferenced boundaries of world countries and cultural regions for use in mapping historical data on global or continental scale · GitHub\",\"url\":\"https://github.com/aourednik/historical-basemaps\"},{\"title\":\"Set Project CRS | Georeferencing Historical Maps\",\"url\":\"https://ubc-library-rc.github.io/gis-georeferencing/content/projections.html\"},{\"title\":\"Coordinate Reference System and Spatial Projection | Earth Data Science - Earth Lab\",\"url\":\"https://earthdatascience.org/courses/earth-analytics/spatial-data-r/intro-to-coordinate-reference-systems/\"},{\"title\":\"Introduction to Geospatial Concepts: Coordinate Reference Systems\",\"url\":\"https://datacarpentry.github.io/organization-geospatial/03-crs.html\"},{\"title\":\"OGR Coordinate Reference Systems and Coordinate Transformation tutorial — GDAL documentation\",\"url\":\"https://gdal.org/en/stable/tutorials/osr_api_tut.html\"},{\"title\":\"Spatial reference system - Wikipedia\",\"url\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spatial_reference_system\"},{\"title\":\"CRS Finder - Intelligent Coordinate Reference System finder and assign projection to the Layer on the fly. This plugin helps users find suitable Coordinate Reference Systems for their …\",\"url\":\"https://plugins.qgis.org/plugins/crs_finder/\"},{\"title\":\"Spatial references | Documentation | Esri Developer\",\"url\":\"https://developers.arcgis.com/documentation/spatial-references/\"},{\"title\":\"Coordinate Reference Systems (CRS)\",\"url\":\"https://rspatial.org/spatial/6-crs.html\"},{\"title\":\"Overview of Coordinate Reference Systems (CRS) in R\",\"url\":\"https://www.nceas.ucsb.edu/sites/default/files/2020-04/OverviewCoordinateReferenceSystems.pdf\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, here's what I found about Aourednik historical basemaps and their coordinate reference system:\n\n## Coordinate Reference System (CRS)\n\nThe geodata in the Aourednik historical basemaps are stored in the WGS 84 projection, EPSG:4326 (crs:OGC:1.3:CRS84). Coordinates are in LatLon, with the projection being geographical.\n\n## Projection Recommendations\n\nThe documentation recommends considering reprojection to show the maps on world scale by choosing a projection with minimal area distortion, such as the Dymaxion (AirOcean) projection or the Molweide projection. Most mapping software and algorithms reproject on the fly.\n\n## Data Format\n\nAll maps are stored in the GeoJSON format with single files per feature layer, which are human and machine readable and easy to import in QGIS or use in D3. Additionally, SVG versions of the maps are created from GeoJSON files using an R script that by default uses a \"Natural Earth\" projection, which can be changed by using another PROJ string.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"719fa47e-696e-417a-a518-5784cb7c2c34","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:34.696Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"d9dc846f-f0e0-475a-b789-1b48d2cd3a90","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

{"parentUuid":"6bf13d6f-2cc2-49f7-8668-685f2decdfa9","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"progress","data":{"type":"hook_progress","hookEvent":"PostToolUse","hookName":"PostToolUse:Read","command":"callback"},"parentToolUseID":"toolu_01FzRkgr5V6DfSejX7zWskQT","toolUseID":"toolu_01FzRkgr5V6DfSejX7zWskQT","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:43.685Z","uuid":"f12479bb-47b6-4554-a0ba-fddc4974f045","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"6bf13d6f-2cc2-49f7-8668-685f2decdfa9","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01FzRkgr5V6DfSejX7zWskQT","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→#' Trade data sources\n     2→#'\n     3→#' @description\n     4→#' Create a new dataframe where each row has a year range into one where each\n     5→#' row is a single year, effectively 'expanding' the whole year range.\n     6→#'\n     7→#' @param trade_sources A tibble dataframe where each row contains the\n     8→#'   year range.\n     9→#'\n    10→#' @returns A tibble dataframe where each row corresponds to a single year for\n    11→#'   a given source.\n    12→#'\n    13→#' @export\n    14→#'\n    15→#' @examples\n    16→#' trade_sources <- tibble::tibble(\n    17→#'   Name = c(\"a\", \"b\", \"c\"),\n    18→#'   Trade = c(\"t1\", \"t2\", \"t3\"),\n    19→#'   Info_Format = c(\"year\", \"partial_series\", \"year\"),\n    20→#'   Timeline_Start = c(1, 1, 2),\n    21→#'   Timeline_End = c(3, 4, 5),\n    22→#'   Timeline_Freq = c(1, 1, 2),\n    23→#'   `Imp/Exp` = \"Imp\",\n    24→#'   SACO_link = NA,\n    25→#' )\n    26→#' expand_trade_sources(trade_sources)\n    27→expand_trade_sources <- function(trade_sources) {\n    28→  non_na_cols <- c(\"Trade\", \"Timeline_Start\", \"Timeline_End\", \"Timeline_Freq\")\n    29→  trade_sources |>\n    30→    dplyr::filter(!.any_na_col(non_na_cols)) |>\n    31→    .expand_trade_years() |>\n    32→    dplyr::mutate(\n    33→      Name = dplyr::if_else(\n    34→        Info_Format == \"year\",\n    35→        paste(Name, Year, sep = \"_\"),\n    36→        Name\n    37→      ),\n    38→      ImpExp = `Imp/Exp`,\n    39→      In_Saco = as.integer(!is.na(SACO_link)),\n    40→    )\n    41→}\n    42→\n    43→.expand_trade_years <- function(trade_sources) {\n    44→  trade_sources <- dplyr::mutate(trade_sources, No = dplyr::row_number())\n    45→\n    46→  trade_sources |>\n    47→    dplyr::group_by(No) |>\n    48→    tidyr::expand(Year = seq(Timeline_Start, Timeline_End, Timeline_Freq)) |>\n    49→    dplyr::inner_join(trade_sources, by = \"No\")\n    50→}\n    51→\n    52→.any_na_col <- function(cols_to_check) {\n    53→  dplyr::if_any(dplyr::all_of(cols_to_check), is.na)\n    54→}\n    55→"}]},"uuid":"c35f0569-0d23-4195-911a-fb406718e10e","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:43.684Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"6bf13d6f-2cc2-49f7-8668-685f2decdfa9","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

{"parentUuid":"df718f59-512e-474c-807f-079809832db5","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01AN7bnzMJWZK24LMeKzcZSY","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"CShapes 2.0 codebook shapefile columns GWCODE COWCODE status type field dependent territory\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"ETH - International Conflict Research - CShapes\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/\"},{\"title\":\"Cshapes 2.0: Documentation\",\"url\":\"https://icr.ethz.ch/data/cshapes/CShapes-2.0_Codebook.pdf\"},{\"title\":\"The CShapes 2.0 Dataset and Utilities\",\"url\":\"https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/cshapes/cshapes.pdf\"},{\"title\":\"https://druid.datalegend.net/nlgis/cshapes/assets/...\",\"url\":\"https://druid.datalegend.net/nlgis/cshapes/assets/5bdb2dc8951bd202aa47cdbb\"},{\"title\":\"Mapping the International System, 1886-2019: The CShapes 2.0 Dataset - Guy Schvitz, Luc Girardin, Seraina Rüegger, Nils B. Weidmann, Lars-Erik Cederman, Kristian Skrede Gleditsch, 2022\",\"url\":\"https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/00220027211013563\"},{\"title\":\"Working with Large Spatial Data in R - Yan Luo\",\"url\":\"https://luoyan407.github.io/posts/2020/09/spatial-sql\"},{\"title\":\"Working with Large Spatial Data in R - Daniel Rojas\",\"url\":\"https://danielrojaslozano.github.io/posts/2020/09/spatial-sql\"},{\"title\":\"Layer: CShapes-2.0 (ID:18)\",\"url\":\"https://services1.arcgis.com/DIcffHalFljSYvfk/arcgis/rest/services/Map_uten_landegrenser_WFL1/FeatureServer/18\"},{\"title\":\"Working with Large Spatial Data in R - Yushang Wei\",\"url\":\"https://yushang1994.github.io/yushangw/posts/2020/09/spatial-sql\"},{\"title\":\"Working with Large Spatial Data in R - Pinjia He\",\"url\":\"https://pinjiahe.github.io/posts/2020/09/spatial-sql\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, I found information about CShapes 2.0 shapefile columns. Here's what I found:\n\n## CShapes 2.0 Shapefile Columns\n\nThe CShapes 2.0 dataset includes the following key columns: CNTRY_NAME (country name), AREA (polygon area in square kilometers), CAPNAME (capital name), CAPLONG and CAPLAT (capital coordinates), FEATUREID (unique identifier), COWCODE (Correlates of War numeric identifier), COWSYEAR/COWSMONTH/COWSDAY (start date based on COW system list), COWEYEAR/COWEMONTH/COWEDAY (end date based on COW system list), and GWCODE (Gleditsch & Ward numeric identifier).\n\nAdditional columns include ISO1NUM (ISO 3166-1 numerical country codes), ISO1AL2 (ISO 3166-1 Alpha2 two-character codes), and ISO1AL3 (ISO 3166-1 Alpha3 three-character codes).\n\n## Data Coverage\n\nCShapes 2.0 maps the borders and capitals of independent states and dependent territories from 1886 to 2019 and from 1816 for CShapes-Europe. There are two versions of the dataset, which are based on the Gleditsch and Ward (1999) or the Correlates of War coding of independent states.\n\n## Missing Value Handling\n\nIt is strongly recommended to drop polygons with missing values (-1) in the respective coding scheme. For example, when working with COW codes, simply drop polygons with COWCODE = -1.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"2cfd374c-01b8-4adb-bcba-4b34f4eb25ea","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:45.959Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"df718f59-512e-474c-807f-079809832db5","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"0f10816b-d9d8-4b47-8990-8f6e96851c13","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"0a5aff59-a20a-4463-aa3e-1f6c17867a5a","agentId":"aab44b1d0aec1f923","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_011Tpfxm3xDNRR5f7HZ4opGr","type":"tool_result","content":"Web search results for query: \"Natural Earth data CRS WGS84 EPSG:4326 shapefile projection specification\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"Natural Earth » Features - Free vector and raster map data at 1:10m, 1:50m, and 1:110m scales\",\"url\":\"https://www.naturalearthdata.com/features/\"},{\"title\":\"Projections — GeoPandas 1.1.3+0.gf5fe3ff.dirty documentation\",\"url\":\"https://geopandas.org/en/stable/docs/user_guide/projections.html\"},{\"title\":\"Understand EPSG, WKT and Other CRS Definition Styles | Earth Data Science - Earth Lab\",\"url\":\"https://earthdatascience.org/courses/use-data-open-source-python/intro-vector-data-python/spatial-data-vector-shapefiles/epsg-proj4-coordinate-reference-system-formats-python/\"},{\"title\":\"Coordinate Reference Systems — R Spatial\",\"url\":\"https://rspatial.org/raster/spatial/6-crs.html\"},{\"title\":\"Overview of Coordinate Reference Systems (CRS) in R\",\"url\":\"https://www.nceas.ucsb.edu/sites/default/files/2020-04/OverviewCoordinateReferenceSystems.pdf\"},{\"title\":\"Chapter 7 Reprojecting geographic data | Geocomputation with R\",\"url\":\"https://r.geocompx.org/reproj-geo-data\"},{\"title\":\"WGS 84 - WGS84 - World Geodetic System 1984, used in GPS - EPSG:4326\",\"url\":\"https://epsg.io/4326\"},{\"title\":\"GIS in R: Understand EPSG, WKT and other CRS definition styles | Earth Data Science - Earth Lab\",\"url\":\"https://earthdatascience.org/courses/earth-analytics/spatial-data-r/understand-epsg-wkt-and-other-crs-definition-file-types/\"},{\"title\":\"Map projections\",\"url\":\"https://autogis-site.readthedocs.io/en/2021/notebooks/L2/02-projections.html\"},{\"title\":\"Natural Earth » Blog Archive » Topic: Reprojecting Natural Earth - Free vector and raster map data at 1:10m, 1:50m, and 1:110m scales\",\"url\":\"https://www.naturalearthdata.com/forums/topic/reprojecting-natural-earth/\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Natural Earth data's CRS specification:\n\n## Natural Earth Data CRS Specification\n\nNatural Earth's WGS84 projection is specified in shapefile's PRJ file. The CRS can be described as a PROJ4 code EPSG:4326 string \"+proj=longlat +ellps=WGS84 +datum=WGS84 +no_defs\".\n\nAll Natural Earth data use the Geographic coordinate system (projection), WGS84 datum. This is the standard WGS84 (World Geodetic System 1984) geographic coordinate system, which is a latitude/longitude projection in decimal degrees.\n\n### Key Details:\n\n- **EPSG Code:** EPSG:4326\n- **Name:** WGS 84\n- **Projection Type:** Geographic (latitude/longitude)\n- **Datum:** World Geodetic System 1984\n- **Ellipsoid:** WGS 84\n- **Prime Meridian:** Greenwich\n- **Units:** Decimal degrees\n\nThe projection information is stored in the `.prj` file that accompanies Natural Earth shapefiles, which is a standard practice for ArcGIS and similar GIS software to define coordinate reference systems for spatial data.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"caa540b3-3218-4923-933c-0fa6c06a9763","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:46.804Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"0f10816b-d9d8-4b47-8990-8f6e96851c13","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

WGS84 - World Geodetic System 1984, used in GPS - EPSG:4326\",\"url\":\"https://epsg.io/4326\"},{\"title\":\"Re: [udig-users] Reprojection WGS84 (4326) -> WGS84 Mercator / Google\",\"url\":\"https://dev.locationtech.org/mhonarc/lists/udig-users/msg00966.html\"},{\"title\":\"EPSG:4326 WGS 84 -- Spatial Reference\",\"url\":\"https://spatialreference.org/ref/epsg/4326/\"},{\"title\":\"World Geodetic System - Wikipedia\",\"url\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Geodetic_System\"},{\"title\":\"EPSG Geodetic Parameter Dataset - Wikipedia\",\"url\":\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EPSG_Geodetic_Parameter_Dataset\"},{\"title\":\"OGR Coordinate Reference Systems and Coordinate Transformation tutorial — GDAL documentation\",\"url\":\"https://gdal.org/en/stable/tutorials/osr_api_tut.html\"},{\"title\":\"Projections — GeoPandas 1.1.3+0.gf5fe3ff.dirty documentation\",\"url\":\"https://geopandas.org/en/stable/docs/user_guide/projections.html\"},{\"title\":\"Getting Started - pyproj 3.7.2 documentation\",\"url\":\"https://pyproj4.github.io/pyproj/stable/examples.html\"},{\"title\":\"WGS 84\",\"url\":\"https://epsg.org/crs_4326/WGS-84.html\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, here's information about the coordinate reference system specifications you asked about:\n\n## EPSG:4326 - WGS84 Coordinate Reference System\n\nEPSG:4326 is the WGS 84 datum ensemble for 2D (latitude, longitude) coordinates with 2 meter accuracy, used by the Global Positioning System among others.\n\n### Technical Details\n\nEPSG:4326 is classified as a GEOGRAPHIC_2D_CRS with WGS84 Bounds of -180.0, -90.0, 180.0, 90.0, and it uses an ellipsoidal 2D coordinate system with axes of latitude and longitude, with orientations of north and east respectively.\n\n### GADM and WGS84\n\nWhile the search results don't contain specific information about GADM version 4's CRS specifications, the results do show historical references to GADM data. The search results indicate that GADM data from earlier versions (v1) was available in shapefile format and used WGS84 as its coordinate reference system.\n\nFor the most current information about GADM version 4's specific coordinate reference system implementation, you may want to consult the official GADM documentation or metadata files directly, as this information was not available in the search results.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"bb63d9dc-e32e-4e22-9bcd-fa2a070f334a","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:47.543Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"e48c1418-ffca-4ce6-84c5-800bfdfc1dcc","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}

Let me provide you with what I found:\n\n## Web Search Results Summary\n\nCShapes 2.0 is a GIS dataset of country borders covering 1886 to present day, and the latest version (2.0) of the raw CShapes data is available in multiple formats including Shapefile.\n\nRegarding shapefile geometry specifications more broadly, the shapefile specification requires that all non-Null shapes in a shapefile must be of the same shape type. This means a shapefile can contain either POLYGON or MULTIPOLYGON geometries, but not a mix of both.\n\nThe search results contain general information about shapefile geometry types and the CShapes 2.0 dataset itself, but do not return specific technical documentation explicitly stating whether the CShapes 2.0 shapefile uses POLYGON or MULTIPOLYGON geometry types, nor specific WGS84 coordinate system details beyond what is standard for geographic data.\n\nTo get the specific geometry type information you're looking for, you may need to:\n1. Check the official CShapes 2.0 documentation at ETH's International Conflict Research center\n2. Inspect the shapefile directly using a GIS tool or Python library (like Fiona or GeoPandas)\n3. Review the metadata files that accompany the downloaded CShapes shapefile\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."}]},"uuid":"24f1977f-3f1e-412f-b03d-5fd59727bc17","timestamp":"2026-03-24T21:13:52.970Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"12a824d0-fcfa-4dc7-b4df-6a863cd81fa7","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
